title: A Love For Winged Things.
content:
[XXXVII., April 12, 1840.]


    My love flowed e'er for things with wings.
    When boy I sought for forest fowl,
    And caged them in rude rushes' mesh,
    And fed them with my breakfast roll;
    So that, though fragile were the door,
    They rarely fled, and even then
    Would flutter back at faintest call!

    Man-grown, I charm for men.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Victor-Marie Hugo represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Victor-Marie Hugo's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
